content:
You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

# Phase 1: Analyze the Fix

This is a patch for IR:

[Verifier] Add checks for loop metadata nodes
This patch adds checks in verifier to error if loop metadata enable and disable nodes are not following the syntax as per LangRef.

Particualrly, the below metadata must have name and i1.
    "llvm.loop.distribute.enable"
    "llvm.loop.vectorize.enable"

e.g.
!0 = !{!"llvm.loop.distribute.enable", i1 0}

and the below ones should have just the name.
    "llvm.loop.unroll.disable"
    "llvm.loop.unroll.enable"
    "llvm.loop.unroll.full"
    "llvm.loop.unroll_and_jam.enable"
    "llvm.loop.unroll_and_jam.disable"

e.g.
    !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.unroll.disable"}

This patch also fixes inconsistencies in the exisiting tests and adds malformed IR tests which should not go beyond verifier.

Fixes #156685
diff --git a/llvm/lib/IR/Verifier.cpp b/llvm/lib/IR/Verifier.cpp
index f986f5406b2b3..8b4058e34f787 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/IR/Verifier.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/IR/Verifier.cpp
@@ -546,6 +546,7 @@ class Verifier : public InstVisitor<Verifier>, VerifierSupport {
   void visitAccessGroupMetadata(const MDNode *MD);
   void visitCapturesMetadata(Instruction &I, const MDNode *Captures);
   void visitAllocTokenMetadata(Instruction &I, MDNode *MD);
+  void verifyLoopMetadata(const MDNode *LoopID);
 
   template <class Ty> bool isValidMetadataArray(const MDTuple &N);
 #define HANDLE_SPECIALIZED_MDNODE_LEAF(CLASS) void visit##CLASS(const CLASS &N);
@@ -1145,6 +1146,60 @@ void Verifier::visitMDNode(const MDNode &MD, AreDebugLocsAllowed AllowLocs) {
   Check(MD.isResolved(), "All nodes should be resolved!", &MD);
 }
 
+// Loop attributes that require exactly two operands (name + i1).
+static const StringRef LoopBooleanAttributeNames[] = {
+    "llvm.loop.distribute.enable",
+    "llvm.loop.vectorize.enable",
+};
+
+// Loop attributes that allow only a single operand (just the name).
+// TODO: This is not an exhaustive list and can also include attributes
+//       like llvm.loop.licm_versioning.disable.
+static const StringRef LoopSingleOperandAttributeNames[] = {
+    "llvm.loop.unroll.disable",
+    "llvm.loop.unroll.enable",
+    "llvm.loop.unroll.full",
+    "llvm.loop.unroll_and_jam.enable",
+    "llvm.loop.unroll_and_jam.disable",
+};
+
+void Verifier::verifyLoopMetadata(const MDNode *LoopID) {
+  // Allow empty loop metadata (!{}) so the verifier does not crash on malformed
+  // IR; skip further checks.
+  if (LoopID->getNumOperands() == 0)
+    return;
+  Check(LoopID->getNumOperands() >= 1,
+        "llvm.loop metadata must have at least one operand.", LoopID);
+  for (const MDOperand &Op : llvm::drop_begin(LoopID->operands())) {
+    MDNode *Option = dyn_cast<MDNode>(Op);
+    if (!Option || Option->getNumOperands() < 1)
+      continue;
+    MDString *Name = dyn_cast<MDString>(Option->getOperand(0));
+    if (!Name)
+      continue;
+    StringRef AttrName = Name->getString();
+
+    // Verify single-operand attributes.
+    if (llvm::is_contained(LoopSingleOperandAttributeNames, AttrName)) {
+      Check(Option->getNumOperands() == 1,
+            "llvm.loop metadata must have exactly one operand.", Option);
+      continue;
+    }
+
+    // Verify boolean attributes.
+    if (llvm::is_contained(LoopBooleanAttributeNames, AttrName)) {
+      // Boolean loop attribute: exactly 2 operands (name + i1).
+      Check(Option->getNumOperands() == 2,
+            "llvm.loop metadata must have exactly two operands.", Option);
+      auto *Val =
+          mdconst::dyn_extract_or_null<ConstantInt>(Option->getOperand(1));
+      Check(Val && Val->getType()->isIntegerTy(1),
+            "llvm.loop metadata's second operand must be of type i1.", Option);
+      continue;
+    }
+  }
+}
+
 void Verifier::visitValueAsMetadata(const ValueAsMetadata &MD, Function *F) {
   Check(MD.getValue(), "Expected valid value", &MD);
   Check(!MD.getValue()->getType()->isMetadataTy(),
@@ -5831,6 +5886,8 @@ void Verifier::visitInstruction(Instruction &I) {
             ? AreDebugLocsAllowed::Yes
             : AreDebugLocsAllowed::No;
     visitMDNode(*Attachment.second, AllowLocs);
+    if (Kind == LLVMContext::MD_loop)
+      verifyLoopMetadata(Attachment.second);
   }
 
   InstsInThisBlock.insert(&I);


---

## Subsystem Knowledge for IR

For reference, here are some key points about the IR you should consider (but do not limit to these) in your analysis:

# Subsystem Knowledge for IR

## Elements Frequently Missed

*   **1-Bit Integer Signed Ranges and Overflow Semantics**: The compiler frequently misses the extreme boundary conditions of 1-bit integers (`i1`). Specifically, it fails to account for the fact that the representable signed range for `i1` is only `[-1, 0]`, meaning that operations like multiplying by `1` (which is represented as `-1`) or adding `1` to a maximum absolute value will inherently cause signed overflows.
*   **Poison-Generating Flags on Non-Trapping Instructions**: Optimization passes often overlook poison-generating flags (such as `samesign` on `icmp` instructions). While the base instruction might be safe to execute speculatively, the attached flag introduces conditional poison semantics that are missed during control-flow simplification.
*   **Inactive Vector Lanes in Conditional Contexts**: The compiler misses the distinction between active and inactive vector lanes when evaluating undefined behavior (like division by zero). It fails to recognize that a zero or `undef` element in a constant vector might be dynamically masked out by a `select` condition.
*   **Internal Representations of Same-Width Floating-Point Types**: The semantic differences between floating-point types of the exact same bit width (e.g., `bfloat` vs. `half`) are frequently missed. The compiler erroneously treats them as interchangeable during bitcast elimination, ignoring their distinct allocations for exponent and mantissa bits.

## Patterns Not Well Handled

### Pattern 1: Eager Evaluation and Hoisting Across Conditional Boundaries
The optimization pass struggles with threading, hoisting, or simplifying operations that are guarded by conditional contexts (like `select` instructions or conditional branches). When an operation contains potential undefined behavior (e.g., vector division by zero in an inactive lane) or poison-generating flags (e.g., `icmp samesign`), the compiler eagerly evaluates the operation unconditionally. By bypassing the original condition that would have masked the faulting lane or prevented the poison generation, the compiler incorrectly propagates `poison` or undefined behavior to the final result, leading to miscompilations.

### Pattern 2: Flawed Constant Range and Flag Inference for 1-Bit Integers
High-level analyses, such as Correlated Value Propagation and constant range calculation, do not well handle the mathematical edge cases of 1-bit arithmetic. The compiler applies standard integer optimization rules—such as assuming `x * 1` cannot overflow, or calculating the upper bound of an absolute value by adding one to the maximum value—without respecting the `[-1, 0]` signed boundary of `i1` types. This leads to incorrect flag inference (like erroneously attaching `nsw`) and wrapped-around, empty constant ranges, which subsequently cause valid instructions to be folded into incorrect constants.

### Pattern 3: Invalid Elimination of Bitcasts Between Semantically Distinct Same-Width Types
The compiler's cast-tracking and simplification logic mishandles sequences involving `bitcast` instructions followed by extension or truncation operations. It operates on the flawed high-level assumption that a `bitcast` between any two types of the same size is a semantic no-op and can be safely bypassed. When this pattern involves types with different internal binary representations (such as casting `bfloat` to `half` before an `fpext`), stripping the `bitcast` fundamentally alters the interpreted numerical value of the data, causing subsequent floating-point operations to compute incorrect results.

**CRITICAL**: Subsystem knowledge is **ONLY** a reference for you to understand this component.
Do NOT start your analysis from these patterns. Start from the patch semantics. Use these **ONLY** to cross-check your reasoning.

## Your Task for Phase 1

In this phase, you will review and analyze the fix above carefully and identify potential issues, edge cases, or gaps that the fix might miss. According to the analysis, propose mutation strategies for exposing the issue. These strategies will be used in Phase 2 to mutate existing test cases to target the identified issues.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Context Gathering
In this step, you should gather enough context to construct semantics model of this fix for future analysis. Your goal is to understand the complete flow of this optimization. To achieve this, you should look at the nearby code regions, or try to find similar code regions in the LLVM codebase to understand the semantics of this fix.

You can use search tools:
- Use the `find` and `list` tools to find relevant files in the LLVM code.
- Use the `read` and `grep` tools to understand the context of the fix.

### Step 2: Fix Segmentation and Analysis
Based on the model built for this fix, you should segment the fix into smaller parts and analyze the semantics of each part. Follow this structured workflow for segmentation and analysis:

- **Segmentation**: Break down the fix into smaller segments or key changes, especially focusing on code regions related to semantics changes.
- **Analysis**: For each segment, analyze the semantics and identify any assumptions or preconditions that the fix relies on.

#### Tips for this step
- You can use the `langref` tool to query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- You can pay attention to the commit message and annotations in the code, such as comments or specific patterns that indicate important semantics or assumptions.

### Step 3: Formal Semantic Modeling
- Define the exact semantic contract that this fix claims to satisfy.
- Explicitly list the preconditions under which the contract is assumed to hold.
- Identify how these preconditions may be violated.

### Step 4: Identify Potential Issues and Propose Test Strategies
Based on your analysis, propose around 5 specific test strategies that can be used to expose the issue. When proposing test strategies, consider the following:
- **Focus**: Focus on the assumptions and preconditions you identified.
- **Scenarios**: Think about specific input characteristics or scenarios that could violate these assumptions.
- **Mutations**: Propose mutations that can be applied to existing test cases to create new test cases targeting these scenarios.

Once you have analyzed the fix and identified potential issues or edge cases, use the `stop` tool to specify proposed test strategies along with detailed reasoning. 
Follow this structure for reasoning:

### 1. Fix Understanding
- What optimization or transformation does this fix enable or correct?
- What are the key changes made in the patch?

### 2. Formal Semantic Modeling
- What is the exact semantic function before the fix?
- What is the exact semantic function after the fix?
- Under what conditions might they differ?
- Provide at least one concrete numerical instantiation to illustrate the difference.

### 3. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified
- What assumptions does the fix make about the input?
- What preconditions must hold for this fix to be correct?

### 4. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions
For each assumption and precondition identified, describe specific scenarios or input characteristics that could violate them. The potential cases should only focus on the semantics related to the analysis above.

### 5. Test Strategies
For each potential issue you identified, propose specific test strategies with the following structure:

- Name: [Name]
- Target: [What to mutate]
- Rationale: [Why this might expose an issue]
- Expected Issue: [What incorrect behavior might occur]

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `langref`: Query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- `stop`: End Phase 1 by submitting the identified issues and proposed test strategies.

So it doesn't check the first operand at all!
Wait, is the first operand ALWAYS a self-reference?
In LLVM, `MD_loop` metadata usually has a self-reference as the first operand to ensure it is distinct and to identify the loop.
If the first operand is NOT a self-reference, but instead an attribute like `!{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.enable", i1 1}`, it will be SKIPPED!
Let's check if this is possible.
```cpp
!0 = !{!1}
!1 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.enable", i1 1}
```

20371-
20372-Horizontal reductions of vectors can be expressed using the following
20373-intrinsics. Each one takes a vector operand as an input and applies its
20374-respective operation across all elements of the vector, returning a single
20375-scalar result of the same element type.

24981-
24982-Syntax:
24983-"""""""
24984-This is an overloaded intrinsic.
24985-
24986-::
24987-
24988:      declare <4 x i1> @llvm.loop.dependence.raw.mask.v4i1(ptr %ptrA, ptr %ptrB, i64 immarg %elementSize)
24989:      declare <8 x i1> @llvm.loop.dependence.raw.mask.v8i1(ptr %ptrA, ptr %ptrB, i64 immarg %elementSize)
24990:      declare <16 x i1> @llvm.loop.dependence.raw.mask.v16i1(ptr %ptrA, ptr %ptrB, i64 immarg %elementSize)

24993-
24994-Overview:
24995-"""""""""
24996-
24997-Given a vector store to %ptrA followed by a vector load from %ptrB, this
24998-instruction generates a mask where an active lane indicates that the
24999-read-after-write sequence can be performed safely for that lane, without a
25000-read-after-write hazard or a store-to-load forwarding hazard being introduced.
25001-
25002-A read-after-write hazard occurs when a read-after-write sequence for a given
25003-lane in a vector ends up being executed as a write-after-read sequence due to
25004-the aliasing of pointers.
25005-
25006-A store-to-load forwarding hazard occurs when a vector store writes to an
25007-address that partially overlaps with the address of a subsequent vector load,
25008-meaning that the vector load can't be performed until the vector store is
25009-complete.

25038:      %loop.dependence.mask = call <4 x i1> @llvm.loop.dependence.raw.mask.v4i1(ptr %ptrA, ptr %ptrB, i64 4)
25039-      call @llvm.masked.store.v4i32.p0v4i32(<4 x i32> %vecA, ptr align 4 %ptrA, <4 x i1> %loop.dependence.mask)
25040-      [...]
25041-      %vecB = call <4 x i32> @llvm.masked.load.v4i32.p0v4i32(ptr align 4 %ptrB, <4 x i1> %loop.dependence.mask, <4 x i32> poison)

25043-      ; For the above example, consider the following cases:
25044-      ;
25045-      ; 1. ptrA == ptrB
25046-      ;
25047-      ;  store = <0,1,2,3>       ; array[i] = store;
25048-      ;   load = <0,1,2,3>       ; uint32_t load = array[i];
25049-      ;
25050-      ; This results in a all-true mask. There is no conflict.
25051-      ;
25052-      ; 2. ptrB - ptrA = 2 * elementSize
25053-      ;
25054-      ;  store =  <0,1,2,3>      ; array[i] = store;
25055-      ;   load =      <0,1,2,3>  ; uint32_t load = array[i+2];
25056-      ;

32312:'``llvm.looptrap``' Intrinsic
32313-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
32314-
32315-Syntax:
32316-"""""""
32317-
32318-::
32319-
32320:      declare void @llvm.looptrap() cold noreturn nounwind
32321-
32322-Overview:
32323-"""""""""
32324-
32325:The '``llvm.looptrap``' intrinsic is equivalent to
32326-``llvm.cond.loop(true)``, but is also considered to be ``noreturn``,
32327-which enables certain optimizations by allowing the optimizer to
32328-assume that a branch leading to a call to this intrinsic was not
32329-taken. A late optimization pass will convert this intrinsic to either
32330-``llvm.cond.loop(true)`` or ``llvm.cond.loop(pred)``, where ``pred``

32331-is a predicate for a conditional branch leading to the intrinsic call,
32332-if possible.7704:.. _llvm.loop:
7705-
7706:'``llvm.loop``'
7707-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7708-
7709-It is sometimes useful to attach information to loop constructs. Currently,
7710-loop metadata is implemented as metadata attached to the branch instruction
7711-in the loop latch block. The loop metadata node is a list of
7712-other metadata nodes, each representing a property of the loop. Usually,
7713-the first item of the property node is a string. For example, the
7714:``llvm.loop.unroll.count`` suggests an unroll factor to the loop
7715-unroller:
7716-
7717-.. code-block:: llvm
7718-
7719:      br i1 %exitcond, label %._crit_edge, label %.lr.ph, !llvm.loop !0
7720-    ...
7721-    !0 = !{!0, !1, !2}
7722:    !1 = !{!"llvm.loop.unroll.enable"}
7723:    !2 = !{!"llvm.loop.unroll.count", i32 4}
7724-
7725-For legacy reasons, the first item of a loop metadata node must be a
7726-reference to itself. Before the advent of the 'distinct' keyword, this
7727-forced the preservation of otherwise identical metadata nodes. Since
7728-the loop-metadata node can be attached to multiple nodes, the 'distinct'
7729-keyword has become unnecessary.
7730-
7731-Prior to the property nodes, one or two ``DILocation`` (debug location)
7732-nodes can be present in the list. The first, if present, identifies the
7733-source-code location where the loop begins. The second, if present,
7734-identifies the source-code location where the loop ends.
7735-
7736-Loop metadata nodes cannot be used as unique identifiers. They are
7737-neither persistent for the same loop through transformations nor
7738-necessarily unique to just one loop.
7739-
7740:'``llvm.loop.disable_nonforced``'
7741-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7742-
7743-This metadata disables all optional loop transformations unless
7744-explicitly instructed using other transformation metadata such as
7745:``llvm.loop.unroll.enable``. That is, no heuristic will try to determine
7746-whether a transformation is profitable. The purpose is to avoid that the
7747-loop is transformed to a different loop before an explicitly requested
7748-(forced) transformation is applied. For instance, loop fusion can make
7749-other transformations impossible. Mandatory loop canonicalizations such
7750-as loop rotation are still applied.
7751-
7752:It is recommended to use this metadata in addition to any ``llvm.loop.*``
7753-transformation directive. Also, any loop should have at most one
7754-directive applied to it (and a sequence of transformations built using
7755-followup-attributes). Otherwise, which transformation will be applied
7756-depends on implementation details such as the pass pipeline order.
7757-
7758-See :ref:`transformation-metadata` for details.
7759-
7760:'``llvm.loop.vectorize``' and '``llvm.loop.interleave``'
7761-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7762-
7763:Metadata prefixed with ``llvm.loop.vectorize`` or ``llvm.loop.interleave`` are
7764-used to control per-loop vectorization and interleaving parameters such as
7765-vectorization width and interleave count. These metadata should be used in
7766:conjunction with ``llvm.loop`` loop identification metadata. The
7767:``llvm.loop.vectorize`` and ``llvm.loop.interleave`` metadata are only
7768-optimization hints and the optimizer will only interleave and vectorize loops if
7769:it believes it is safe to do so. The ``llvm.loop.parallel_accesses`` metadata
7770-which contains information about loop-carried memory dependencies can be helpful
7771-in determining the safety of these transformations.
7772-
7773:'``llvm.loop.interleave.count``' Metadata
7774-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7775-
7776-This metadata suggests an interleave count to the loop interleaver.
7777:The first operand is the string ``llvm.loop.interleave.count`` and the
7778-second operand is an integer specifying the interleave count. For
7779-example:
7780-
7781-.. code-block:: llvm
7782-
7783:   !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.interleave.count", i32 4}
7784-
7785:Note that setting ``llvm.loop.interleave.count`` to 1 disables interleaving
7786:multiple iterations of the loop. If ``llvm.loop.interleave.count`` is set to 0
7787-then the interleave count will be determined automatically.
7788-
7789:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.enable``' Metadata
7790-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7791-
7792-This metadata selectively enables or disables vectorization for the loop. The
7793:first operand is the string ``llvm.loop.vectorize.enable`` and the second operand
7794-is a bit. If the bit operand value is 1 vectorization is enabled. A value of
7795-0 disables vectorization:
7796-
7797-.. code-block:: llvm
7798-
7799:   !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.enable", i1 0}
7800:   !1 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.enable", i1 1}
7801-
7802:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.predicate.enable``' Metadata
7803-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7804-
7805-This metadata selectively enables or disables creating predicated instructions
7806-for the loop, which can enable folding of the scalar epilogue loop into the
7807-main loop. The first operand is the string
7808:``llvm.loop.vectorize.predicate.enable`` and the second operand is a bit. If
7809-the bit operand value is 1 vectorization is enabled. A value of 0 disables
7810-vectorization:
7811-
7812-.. code-block:: llvm
7813-
7814:   !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.predicate.enable", i1 0}
7815:   !1 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.predicate.enable", i1 1}
7816-
7817:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.scalable.enable``' Metadata
7818-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7819-
7820-This metadata selectively enables or disables scalable vectorization for the
7821-loop, and only has any effect if vectorization for the loop is already enabled.
7822:The first operand is the string ``llvm.loop.vectorize.scalable.enable``
7823-and the second operand is a bit. If the bit operand value is 1 scalable
7824-vectorization is enabled, whereas a value of 0 reverts to the default fixed
7825-width vectorization:
7826-
7827-.. code-block:: llvm
7828-
7829:   !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.scalable.enable", i1 0}
7830:   !1 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.scalable.enable", i1 1}
7831-
7832:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.width``' Metadata
7833-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7834-
7835-This metadata sets the target width of the vectorizer. The first
7836:operand is the string ``llvm.loop.vectorize.width`` and the second
7837-operand is an integer specifying the width. For example:
7838-
7839-.. code-block:: llvm
7840-
7841:   !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.vectorize.width", i32 4}
7842-
7843:Note that setting ``llvm.loop.vectorize.width`` to 1 disables
7844:vectorization of the loop. If ``llvm.loop.vectorize.width`` is set to
7845-0 or if the loop does not have this metadata the width will be
7846-determined automatically.
7847-
7848:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.followup_vectorized``' Metadata
7849-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7850-
7851-This metadata defines which loop attributes the vectorized loop will
7852-have. See :ref:`transformation-metadata` for details.
7853-
7854:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.followup_epilogue``' Metadata
7855-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7856-
7857-This metadata defines which loop attributes the epilogue will have. The
7858-epilogue is not vectorized and is executed when either the vectorized
7859-loop is not known to preserve semantics (because e.g., it processes two
7860-arrays that are found to alias by a runtime check) or for the last
7861-iterations that do not fill a complete set of vector lanes. See
7862-:ref:`Transformation Metadata <transformation-metadata>` for details.
7863-
7864:'``llvm.loop.vectorize.followup_all``' Metadata
7865-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7866-
7867-Attributes in the metadata will be added to both the vectorized and
7868-epilogue loop.
7869-See :ref:`Transformation Metadata <transformation-metadata>` for details.

7871:'``llvm.loop.unroll``'
7872-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7873-
7874:Metadata prefixed with ``llvm.loop.unroll`` are loop unrolling
7875:optimization hints such as the unroll factor. ``llvm.loop.unroll``
7876:metadata should be used in conjunction with ``llvm.loop`` loop
7877:identification metadata. The ``llvm.loop.unroll`` metadata are only
7878-optimization hints and the unrolling will only be performed if the
7879-optimizer believes it is safe to do so.

7881:'``llvm.loop.unroll.count``' Metadata
7882-^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
7883-
7884-This metadata suggests an unroll factor to the loop unroller. The
7885:first operand is the string ``llvm.loop.unroll.count`` and the second
7886-operand is a positive integer specifying the unroll factor. For
7887-example:
7888-
7889-.. code-block:: llvm
7890-
7891:   !0 = !{!"llvm.loop.unroll.count", i32 4}
7892-
7893-If the trip count of the loop is less than the unroll count the loop
7894-will be partially unrolled.
